topic: HL Extension Topics and Quantitative Analysis
subject: economics

items.q: What is asymmetric information as a source of market failure?
items.correct: When one party in a transaction knows more than the other — leading to problems like adverse selection (used-car market) or moral hazard (insurance). Markets can fail or unravel when information is unequal.
items.misconception: Asymmetric information means a buyer and seller disagree about a price.
items.random_wrong: Asymmetric information is when a market only operates in one direction.
items.why: Disagreement about value is normal trade. Asymmetric information is about one side genuinely knowing more — Akerlof's 'lemons' problem shows how this can destroy entire markets. Students collapse the concept into ordinary haggling.

items.q: What did the original Phillips curve suggest, and how has thinking changed?
items.correct: The original Phillips curve suggested a stable inverse relationship between unemployment and inflation. Friedman and the 1970s stagflation experience added expectations — the long-run Phillips curve is vertical at the natural rate of unemployment.
items.misconception: The Phillips curve still proves you can always reduce unemployment by allowing more inflation.
items.random_wrong: The Phillips curve shows the link between phone use and unemployment.
items.why: The simple inverse trade-off broke down empirically. Modern theory distinguishes short-run (trade-off possible) from long-run (no trade-off). Students learn the original curve as if still valid.

items.q: What is the technical definition of a recession?
items.correct: Two consecutive quarters of negative real GDP growth — a contraction of the economy.
items.misconception: A recession is when prices fall and people lose jobs.
items.random_wrong: A recession is when the stock market crashes.
items.why: Job losses and stock falls often accompany recessions but don't define them. The technical definition is GDP-based. Students conflate symptoms (unemployment) with definition (negative growth).

items.q: Alfred Marshall published 'Principles of Economics' establishing supply-demand analysis in:
items.why: It's 1890. “1776” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: A fall in the price of a substitute good causes demand to:
items.why: Correct answer: Decrease (curve shifts left). “Increase (shifts right)” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
The real figure is 1890 AD. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
